name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
وَأَنَّ ٱلْمَسَـٰجِدَ لِلَّهِ فَلَا تَدْعُوا۟ مَعَ ٱللَّهِ أَحَدًا (١٨)
```

```english
The places of worship belong to Allah, so do not invoke anyone along with Allah. (18)
```

```arabic
وَأَنَّهُۥ لَمَّا قَامَ عَبْدُ ٱللَّهِ يَدْعُوهُ كَادُوا۟ يَكُونُونَ عَلَيْهِ لِبَدًا (١٩)
```

```english
When the servant of Allah rose to pray to Him, they almost crowded around him. (19)
```

```arabic
قُلْ إِنَّمَآ أَدْعُوا۟ رَبِّى وَلَآ أُشْرِكُ بِهِۦٓ أَحَدًا (٢٠)
```

```english
Say, ‘I pray only to my Lord, and I do not ascribe any partner to Him.’ (20)
```

```arabic
قُلْ إِنِّى لَآ أَمْلِكُ لَكُمْ ضَرًّا وَلَا رَشَدًا (٢١)
```

```english
Say, ‘I have no power to bring you any harm or good [of my own accord].’ (21)
```

```arabic
قُلْ إِنِّى لَن يُجِيرَنِى مِنَ ٱللَّهِ أَحَدٌ وَلَنْ أَجِدَ مِن دُونِهِۦ مُلْتَحَدًا (٢٢)
```

```english
Say, ‘Neither can anyone shelter me from Allah, nor can I find any refuge besides Him. (22)
```

```arabic
إِلَّا بَلَـٰغًا مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ وَرِسَـٰلَـٰتِهِۦ ۚ وَمَن يَعْصِ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥ فَإِنَّ لَهُۥ نَارَ جَهَنَّمَ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَآ أَبَدًا (٢٣)
```

```english
[I have no duty] except to transmit from Allah, and [to communicate] His messages; and whoever disobeys Allah and His apostle, there will indeed be for him the fire of hell, to remain in it forever.’ (23)
```

```arabic
حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا رَأَوْا۟ مَا يُوعَدُونَ فَسَيَعْلَمُونَ مَنْ أَضْعَفُ نَاصِرًا وَأَقَلُّ عَدَدًا (٢٤)
```

```english
When they see what they are promised, they will know who is weaker in supporters and fewer in numbers. (24)
```

```arabic
قُلْ إِنْ أَدْرِىٓ أَقَرِيبٌ مَّا تُوعَدُونَ أَمْ يَجْعَلُ لَهُۥ رَبِّىٓ أَمَدًا (٢٥)
```

```english
Say, ‘I do not know if what you are promised is near, or if my Lord has set a [long] term for it.’ (25)
```

```arabic
عَـٰلِمُ ٱلْغَيْبِ فَلَا يُظْهِرُ عَلَىٰ غَيْبِهِۦٓ أَحَدًا (٢٦)
```

```english
Knower of the Unseen, He does not disclose His [knowledge of the] Unseen to anyone, (26)
```

```arabic
إِلَّا مَنِ ٱرْتَضَىٰ مِن رَّسُولٍ فَإِنَّهُۥ يَسْلُكُ مِنۢ بَيْنِ يَدَيْهِ وَمِنْ خَلْفِهِۦ رَصَدًا (٢٧)
```

```english
except to an apostle He approves of. Then He dispatches a sentinel before and behind him (27)
```

```arabic
لِّيَعْلَمَ أَن قَدْ أَبْلَغُوا۟ رِسَـٰلَـٰتِ رَبِّهِمْ وَأَحَاطَ بِمَا لَدَيْهِمْ وَأَحْصَىٰ كُلَّ شَىْءٍ عَدَدًۢا (٢٨)
```

```english
so that He may ascertain that they have communicated the messages of their Lord, and He encompasses all that is with them, and He keeps an count of all things. (28)
```

## (Commentary)

The verses serve to establish the prophethood (النبوة), mention His oneness (وحدانيته), and the Return (المعاد) — *the resurrection and final judgment* — as a conclusion drawn from the narrative, and they conclude by pointing toward the inerrancy (عصمة) — *the divine protection from error and sin* — of the Message.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَأَنَّ الْمَساجِدَ لِلَّهِ فَلا تَدْعُوا مَعَ اللهِ أَحَداً** (And that the mosques belong to Allah, so do not invoke anyone along with Allah) [72:18] is coordinated with His saying: **أَنَّهُ اسْتَمَعَ** (that... listened) [72:1] etc. The clause "that the mosques belong to Allah" stands in the position of providing the rationale for His saying: "so do not invoke anyone along with Allah." The underlying meaning is: do not invoke anyone other than Allah along with Him, because the mosques belong to Him.

What is meant by "invocation" (الدعاء) is worship (العبادة), and Allah has named it invocation as in His saying: **وَقالَ رَبُّكُمُ ادْعُونِي أَسْتَجِبْ لَكُمْ إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ عَنْ عِبادَتِي سَيَدْخُلُونَ جَهَنَّمَ داخِرِينَ** (Your Lord has said, ‘Call Me, and I will hear you!’ Indeed those who are too proud to worship Me will enter hell, abased) [40:60].

There is disagreement regarding what is meant by "mosques" (المساجد). It has been said that it refers to the Kaaba (الكعبة); it has been said it refers to the Sacred Mosque (المسجد الحرام); and it has been said it refers to the Sacred Mosque and the Furthest Mosque (بيت المقدس). These views are refuted by the fact that "mosques" (المساجد) is a plural form that does not apply to one or two.

It has been said it refers to the Sanctuary (الحرم), which is a baseless claim (تهكم) lacking evidence. It has also been said it refers to the entire earth, based on his saying (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): "The earth has been made for me a place of prostration (مسجداً) and a means of purification." The flaw in this is that the hadith only indicates the permissibility of worship in any spot of the earth, contrary to what is known of the Jews and Christians regarding the impermissibility of worshipping the Exalted outside of synagogues and churches. As for naming all spots of the earth "mosques" such that the term would carry that meaning when used absolutely, that is not the case.

It has been said that it refers to the prayers (الصلوات), meaning one should not pray except to Allah; this is also a baseless claim without evidence.

It is narrated from Imam al-Jawad (عليه السلام) that the "mosques" refer to the seven organs upon which one prostrates during prayer: the forehead, the two palms, the two knees, and the toes of the two feet. His narration will be presented in the following "Discussion on Traditions," God willing. This has also been reported from Sa'id ibn Jubayr, al-Farra, and al-Zajjaj.

Based on this, it is most appropriate that the meaning of these places of prostration in a human being "belonging to Allah" is their legislative exclusivity (اختصاصها التشريعي) to Him. The "invocation" (الدعاء) then refers to prostration (السجدة), as it is the most manifest instance of worship, or to the prayer (الصلاة) insofar as it comprises prostration to Allah, the Glorified.

The meaning would be: "And it was revealed to me that the organs of prostration are exclusive to Allah the Exalted, so prostrate to Him with them—or worship Him with them—and do not prostrate to—or worship—anyone other than Him."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَأَنَّهُ لَمَّا قامَ عَبْدُ اللهِ يَدْعُوهُ كادُوا يَكُونُونَ عَلَيْهِ لِبَداً** (When the servant of Allah rose to pray to Him, they almost stifled him [in their eagerness to hear him]) [72:19]. The word *libad* (لِبَداً) is the plural of *lubdah* (لُبْدَة), referring to things gathered and piled upon one another. The "servant of Allah" refers to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), as indicated by the following verse. The use of the expression "servant of Allah" serves as a prelude to His saying in the next verse: "Say, 'I only pray to my Lord.'" It is most fitting for the context of the subsequent verses that the referent of the plural pronouns in His saying "they almost were" (كادُوا يَكُونُونَ) be the polytheists (المشركين). They used to crowd around him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) when he prayed and recited the Quran, mocking him and raising their voices above his, according to what has been narrated.

The meaning is: "And that when the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) rose to worship Allah through prayer, the polytheists, in their crowding, almost became a dense, piled-up mass around him."

It has been said that the pronouns refer to the jinn (الجن), and that they gathered around him and crowded together, looking at him in wonder at what they witnessed of his worship and his recitation of a Quran the likes of which they had never heard.

It has also been said that the pronouns refer to the believers (المؤمنين) in the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), who gathered around him to follow him in his prayer and to listen to what he recited of the speech of Allah.

However, these two views do not harmonize with the context of the subsequent verses as well as the first view, as previously indicated.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قُلْ إِنَّما أَدْعُوا رَبِّي وَلا أُشْرِكُ بِهِ أَحَداً** (Say, ‘I pray only to my Lord, and I do not ascribe any partner to Him’) [72:20]. This is a command from the Exalted to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to clarify the nature of his worship to them—a clarification that removes their confusion, as they saw from him what they had not seen from anyone else. They were wondering, attributing his actions to some kind of plot or ruse against their idols, or a deception for other worldly purposes.

The summary of the clarification is: "I do not intend by these actions any of the objectives you imagine or accuse me of; rather, I only invoke my Lord alone, without associating anyone with Him. A human being's worship of the One he recognizes as his Lord is not something for which he should be blamed or at which one should wonder."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قُلْ إِنِّي لا أَمْلِكُ لَكُمْ ضَرًّا وَلا رَشَداً** (Say, ‘I have no power to bring you either any harm or any benefit’) [72:21]. What the context of the noble verses suggests is that he (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) is clarifying, by the command of his Lord, his own position in relation to his Lord and in relation to the people.

As for his position in relation to his Lord, it is that he invokes Him and does not associate anyone with Him, which is His saying: "Say, 'I only pray to my Lord, and I do not ascribe any partner to Him.'"

As for his position in relation to them, it is that he is a mortal like them; he possesses for them neither harm (ضراً) nor right guidance (رشداً) — *the state of being guided to the truth and the correct path* — such that he could harm them to force them toward the good he wishes to guide them to, using any power of his own. Furthermore, he is commanded by Allah to call them—a command he has no choice but to obey. No protector could protect him from Allah, nor could he find any refuge to turn to if he were to disagree or disobey, just as they have no choice but to obey Allah and His Messenger. "And whoever disobeys Allah and His Messenger, indeed for him is the fire of Hell, to abide therein forever." They will soon know when they see what they are promised.

The implication of this context is that "possessing harm" (ملك الضر) means the power to inflict harm upon them whenever he wishes, and "possessing right guidance" (ملك الرشد) means the power to bring benefit to them by hitting upon the truth. That is: "I do not claim that I am able to harm you or benefit you." It has also been said that "harm" (الضر) here refers to error (الغي), the opposite of right guidance (الرشد), expressing the cause by the name of the effect.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قُلْ إِنِّي لَنْ يُجِيرَنِي مِنَ اللهِ أَحَدٌ وَلَنْ أَجِدَ مِنْ دُونِهِ مُلْتَحَداً إِلَّا بَلاغاً مِنَ اللهِ وَرِسالاتِهِ** (Say, ‘No one can ever protect me from Allah, nor can I ever find any refuge besides Him, except for a delivery from Allah and His messages’) [72:22-23]. "Protection" (الإجارة) is the granting of sanctuary, the effect of which is the protector's defense of the one seeking refuge and preventing anyone from reaching him with harm. It appears that "refuge" (الملتحد) is a noun of place, referring to a place to which one swerves or turns to guard against evil. It has also been said to mean an entrance. The phrase "besides Him" (مِنْ دُونِهِ) relates to it as an explanatory constraint, and the pronoun refers to Allah. "Delivery" (البلاغ) means the act of conveying (التبليغ).

His saying: "except for a delivery" (إِلَّا بَلاغاً) is an exception from "refuge" (مُلْتَحَداً). His saying: "from Allah" (مِنَ اللهِ) relates to an omitted word, meaning "originating from Allah," and is not related to "delivery" (بَلاغاً) because the latter takes the preposition *'an* (عن), not *min* (من). Therefore, some who made it relate to "delivery" said that *min* here means *'an*. In any case, the meaning is: "except for the conveying of that which He, the Exalted, possesses of Names and Attributes."

Regarding His saying: "and His messages" (وَرِسالاتِهِ), it has been said it is coordinated with "delivery" (بَلاغاً), the meaning being: "except for a delivery from Allah and except for His messages." It has also been said it is coordinated with the Majestic Name (Allah), with *min* meaning *'an*, the meaning being: "except for a delivery from Allah and from His messages."

There is another view regarding the exception of "delivery," which is that it is the object of "I do not possess" (لا أَمْلِكُ), meaning: "I possess for you neither harm nor right guidance, except for a delivery from Allah and His messages." This is unlikely due to the separation between the exception and the excepted-from by His saying: "No one can ever protect me from Allah..." etc., which is a new sentence.

The meaning of the two verses, based on what we have presented, is: "Say: No one shall protect me from Allah to prevent Him from reaching me, nor shall I find any place besides Him to take refuge in, except for a delivery originating from Him and His messages." That is: "except that I fulfill what He commanded me of conveying from Him, the Exalted, by clarifying His Names and Attributes, and His messages regarding the laws of the religion."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَمَنْ يَعْصِ اللهَ وَرَسُولَهُ فَإِنَّ لَهُ نارَ جَهَنَّمَ خالِدِينَ فِيها أَبَداً** (And whoever disobeys Allah and His Apostle, indeed there is for him the fire of hell, to remain in it forever) [72:23]. The singular pronoun in "for him" (لَهُ) considers the literal form of "whoever" (مَنْ), while the plural "abiding" (خالِدِينَ) considers its meaning.

The coordination of the Messenger with Allah in His saying: "And whoever disobeys Allah and His Messenger" is because disobedience to him is disobedience to Allah, since he possesses nothing but the message of his Lord. Thus, rejecting him in what he has brought is a rejection of Allah, the Glorified, and obeying him in what he commands is obedience to Allah. The Exalted said: **مَنْ يُطِعِ الرَّسُولَ فَقَدْ أَطاعَ اللهَ** (Whoever obeys the Apostle certainly obeys Allah) [4:80].

What is meant by "disobedience" (المعصية)—as witnessed by the context of the preceding verses—is disobedience to what he commanded of monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — or monotheism and what branches from it of the principles of religion (أصول الدين) and its branches (فروعه). Thus, the threat and the promise of eternal Fire do not include anyone except those who disbelieve in the core of the call, rather than all people of disobedience who fail in the branches of religion. Therefore, using this verse as an argument for the eternal damnation of all sinners in general is misplaced.

It appears that His saying: "And whoever disobeys Allah..." to the end of the verse is the speech of Allah, the Glorified, and not a continuation of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) speech.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **حَتَّى إِذا رَأَوْا ما يُوعَدُونَ فَسَيَعْلَمُونَ مَنْ أَضْعَفُ ناصِراً وَأَقَلُّ عَدَداً** (When they see what they are promised, they will know who is weaker in supporters and fewer in numbers) [72:24]. The word "until" (حَتَّى) indicates that the meaning of what follows it is the goal (غاية) of an omitted preceding clause. What follows it indicates that they used to consider the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and his supporters—the believers—to be weak, and his numbers to be few. Thus, the speech points to an omitted meaning for which it is the goal, such as: "They will continue to deem your supporters weak and your numbers few until they see what they are promised..." etc.

What is meant by "what they are promised" is the fire of Hell, because it is what was promised in the previous verse. The verse is from the speech of the Exalted addressing the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). Had it been from his own speech, introduced by the command "Say" (قُلْ), the phrasing would have been: "Until when you see what you are promised, you will know..." etc.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قُلْ إِنْ أَدْرِي أَقَرِيبٌ ما تُوعَدُونَ أَمْ يَجْعَلُ لَهُ رَبِّي أَمَداً** (Say, ‘I do not know if what you are promised is near, or if my Lord has appointed a term for it’) [72:25]. The "term" (الأمد) is the goal or limit at which something ends. The verse is in the position of repelling an objection necessitated by their state; it is as if when they heard the threat, they said: "When will that be?" So it was said to him: "Say: I do not know if it is near..." etc.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **عالِمُ الْغَيْبِ فَلا يُظْهِرُ عَلى غَيْبِهِ أَحَداً** (Knower of the Unseen, He does not disclose His Unseen to anyone) [72:26]. "Disclosing" (إظهار) something to someone means aiding him with it and giving him mastery over it. "Knower of the Unseen" (عالِمُ الْغَيْبِ) is the predicate of an omitted subject, the estimation being: "He is the Knower of the Unseen." The import of the word, aided by the context, is the exclusivity of the knowledge of the Unseen (الغيب) — *that which lies beyond sensory perception and empirical knowledge* — to Allah the Exalted, while His knowledge encompasses every unseen thing. For this reason, He attributed the Unseen to Himself a second time, saying: "to His Unseen" (عَلى غَيْبِهِ), placing the manifest noun in place of the pronoun to signify exclusivity. Had He said: "He does not disclose to it" (فلا يظهر عليه), it would not have conveyed that meaning.

The meaning is: He is the Knower of every unseen thing with a knowledge exclusive to Him, so He does not grant anyone among people access to the Unseen while it is exclusive to Him. Thus, the import is a universal negation (سلب كلي), even if some insist on it being a partial negation (سلب جزئي), the summary of which would be: "He does not disclose all of His Unseen to anyone." What we have said is supported by the apparent meaning of the verses that follow.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِلَّا مَنِ ارْتَضى مِنْ رَسُولٍ** (except to an apostle He approves of) [72:27]. This is an exception from His saying: "anyone" (أَحَداً). The phrase "of an apostle" (مِنْ رَسُولٍ) is an explanation of "whom He approves" (مَنِ ارْتَضى). This indicates that Allah the Exalted discloses to His messengers whatever He wills of the Unseen that is exclusive to Him. When this verse is joined with the verses that restrict the knowledge of the Unseen to Allah the Exalted—such as His saying: **وَعِنْدَهُ مَفاتِحُ الْغَيْبِ لا يَعْلَمُها إِلَّا هُوَ** (With Him are the treasures of the Unseen; no one knows them except Him) [6:59], and His saying: **وَلِلَّهِ غَيْبُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ** (To Allah belongs the Unseen of the heavens and the earth) [16:77], and His saying: **قُلْ لا يَعْلَمُ مَنْ فِي السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ الْغَيْبَ إِلَّا اللهُ** (Say, ‘No one in the heavens and the earth knows the Unseen except Allah’) [27:65]—it conveys the meaning of originality (الأصالة) and dependency (التبعية). He, the Exalted, knows the Unseen by His essence (لذاته), while others know it through teaching from Allah.

These verses are similar to the verses concerning "taking the soul" (التوفي), such as His saying: **اللهُ يَتَوَفَّى الْأَنْفُسَ** (Allah takes the souls) [39:42], which indicates restriction, and His saying: **قُلْ يَتَوَفَّاكُمْ مَلَكُ المَوْتِ الَّذِي وُكِّلَ بِكُمْ** (Say, ‘You will be taken away by the angel of death who has been put in charge of you’) [32:11], and His saying: **حَتَّى إِذا جاءَ أَحَدَكُمُ الْمَوْتُ تَوَفَّتْهُ رُسُلُنا** (until when death comes to one of you, Our messengers take him away) [6:61]. Taking the soul is attributed to Him, the Exalted, by way of originality, and to the angels by way of dependency, as they are intermediary causes (أسباب متوسطة) subjugated to Him, the Exalted.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَإِنَّهُ يَسْلُكُ مِنْ بَيْنِ يَدَيْهِ وَمِنْ خَلْفِهِ رَصَداً** (then He dispatches a guard before him and behind him) [72:27]—until His saying—**عَدَداً** (in number) [72:28]. The pronoun in "indeed He" (فَإِنَّهُ) refers to Allah the Exalted, and the pronouns in "before him" (يَدَيْهِ) and "behind him" (خَلْفِهِ) refer to the Messenger. The "guard" (الرصد) is the one who monitors and protects a matter; it is used for both the singular and the group, and is originally a verbal noun. What is meant by "before the Messenger" is what is between him and the people to whom he is sent, and "behind him" is what is between him and the source of revelation (مصدر الوحي), which is Allah, the Glorified. This imagery considers the notion of the "Message" (الرسالة) as an imagined extension starting from the Sender (المرسل) and ending at the recipient (المرسل إليه), which the Messenger traverses until he reaches the recipient to deliver his message. The verse describes the path by which the Unseen reaches the Messenger, which are the messages revealed to him, as indicated by His saying: "so that He may know that they have delivered the messages of their Lord."

The meaning is: Allah dispatches between the Messenger and those to whom he is sent, and between the Messenger and the source of revelation, monitors and guardians from among the angels. It is understood that dispatching the guard before and behind him is to protect the revelation from any confusion or alteration through addition or subtraction that might occur from the side of the devils, whether directly or indirectly.

Regarding His saying: **لِيَعْلَمَ أَنْ قَدْ أَبْلَغُوا رِسالاتِ رَبِّهِمْ** (so that He may know that they have delivered the messages of their Lord) [72:28], the pronoun in "so that He may know" (لِيَعْلَمَ) refers to Allah, the Glorified. The pronouns in "they have delivered" (قَدْ أَبْلَغُوا) and "their Lord" (رَبِّهِمْ) refer to "whom" (مَنِ) considering its meaning, or to "messenger" (رسول) considering the genus. What is meant by His knowledge of their delivery of the messages of their Lord is actual knowledge (العلم الفعلي) — *the realization of the delivery in the external world* — similar to His saying: **فَلَيَعْلَمَنَّ اللهُ الَّذِينَ صَدَقُوا وَلَيَعْلَمَنَّ الْكاذِبِينَ** (Allah will surely know those who are truthful, and He will surely know the liars) [29:3], which occurs frequently in His speech.

The clause provides the rationale for dispatching the guard before and behind the Messenger. The meaning is: so that the delivery of the messages of their Lord may be realized—that is, so that His messages reach the people as they are, without change or alteration.

It is possible that the pronouns in "before him and behind him" refer to "His Unseen" (غَيْبِهِ). In that case, the guard would be dispatched before and behind the descending Unseen until it reaches the Messenger. This is weakened by the fact that it does not harmonize with His saying: "so that He may know that they have delivered the messages of their Lord" in the sense previously mentioned, because the Unseen reaching the Messenger safely from the interference of devils does not necessarily entail the knowledge of its delivery to the people.

To this meaning returns the view of some who said the two pronouns refer to Gabriel, the carrier of the revelation. This is weakened, in addition to what has passed, by the lack of prior mention of him.

It has been said that the pronoun in "so that he may know" (ليعلم) refers to the Messenger, and the pronouns in "they have delivered" and "their Lord" refer to the angelic guards. The meaning would be: the angels monitor and guard the revelation so that the Messenger may know that the angels have delivered the revelation to him exactly as it originated, so his soul may be at peace that it is safe from the interference of devils; for the knowledge of their delivery of it necessitates the knowledge of its arrival.

This is unlikely because the apparent context—supported by the prior mention of the Messenger—is that the "messages" (الرسالات) refer to the messages carried by the Messenger to deliver to the people, not those carried by the angel of revelation. Thus, the pronoun in "their Lord" refers to the messengers, not the angels. Furthermore, the verse refers to the angels as "guards" (رصد), which is distinct from the role of "message-bearing" (الرسالة); the function of a guard is protection and monitoring, not delivery.

It has been said the meaning is: so that Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) may know that the messengers before him had delivered the messages of their Lord. This is a weak view with no evidence. Even weaker is the view that the meaning is: so that the one who denies the messengers may know that the messengers have delivered the messages of their Lord to them.

His saying: **وَأَحاطَ بِما لَدَيْهِمْ** (and He encompasses all that is with them) [72:28]. The plural pronoun refers to the messengers based on the preceding meaning. It appears that the clause completes the meaning of the aforementioned protection. His saying: "before him" refers to the guarding of what is between the Messenger and the recipients; His saying: "and behind him" refers to the protection of what is between him and the source of revelation; and His saying: "and He encompasses all that is with them" refers to the internal state of the Messenger himself. This encompassment is an encompassment of knowledge (إحاطة علمية). Thus, the revelation is secure from the intrusion of change and alteration between the source of revelation and the Messenger, within the Messenger himself, and between the Messenger and the recipients.

It is possible that "what is with them" (ما لديهم) refers to everything that has any connection to the messengers, broader than just the path of revelation or their own selves. Similarly, His saying: **وَأَحْصى كُلَّ شَيْءٍ عَدَداً** (and He keeps count of all things) [72:28] is intended to convey the universality of knowledge regarding all things, specifically the knowledge of their number and the distinction of one from another.

From what has passed in these three verses, several points become clear:

First: His exclusivity in the knowledge of the Unseen is by way of originality (الأصالة) in the sense we have clarified: He, the Exalted, knows the Unseen by His essence, while others know it through teaching from Him.

Through this, it becomes clear that where His speech recounts their denial of the knowledge of the Unseen, what is intended is the negation of originality and independence, not what occurs through revelation. Examples include His saying: **قُلْ لا أَقُولُ لَكُمْ عِنْدِي خَزائِنُ اللهِ وَلا أَعْلَمُ الْغَيْبَ** (Say, ‘I do not say to you that I possess the treasuries of Allah, nor do I know the Unseen’) [6:50], and His saying: **وَلَوْ كُنْتُ أَعْلَمُ الْغَيْبَ لَاسْتَكْثَرْتُ مِنَ الْخَيْرِ** (Had I known the Unseen, I would have surely amassed much good) [18:188], and His saying: **قُلْ ما كُنْتُ بِدْعاً مِنَ الرُّسُلِ وَما أَدْرِي ما يُوَدُّ بِي وَلا بِكُمْ إِنْ أَتَّبِعُ إِلَّا ما يُوحى إِلَيَّ** (Say, ‘I am not a novelty among the apostles, nor do I know what will be done with me, or with you. I just follow what is revealed to me’) [46:9].

Second: The generality of His saying: "He does not disclose His Unseen to anyone" (فَلا يُظْهِرُ عَلى غَيْبِهِ أَحَداً), once specified by His saying: "except to an apostle He approves of" (إِلَّا مَنِ ارْتَضى مِنْ رَسُولٍ), becomes a specified generality that does not resist further specification by another specifier (مخصص). This is the case with the prophets (الأنبياء); Quranic verses indicate that they receive revelation, as in His saying: **إِنَّا أَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْكَ كَما أَوْحَيْنا إِلى نُوحٍ وَالنَّبِيِّينَ مِنْ بَعْدِهِ** (We have indeed revealed to you as We revealed to Noah and the prophets after him) [4:163], and they indicate that revelation is from the Unseen. Thus, the prophet (النبي) attains the Unseen just as the messenger (الرسول) does. This is assuming "messenger" in the verse is meant as the counterpart to "prophet." However, if it refers to anyone sent by Allah to the people—and the prophet is among those sent by Allah to them, as witnessed by His saying: **وَما أَرْسَلْنا مِنْ قَبْلِكَ مِنْ رَسُولٍ وَلا نَبِيٍّ** (We did not send any apostle or prophet before you...) [22:52] and His saying: **وَما أَرْسَلْنا فِي قَرْيَةٍ مِنْ نَبِيٍّ** (We did not send a prophet to any town...) [7:94]—then the prophet is excluded from the general negation without needing a new specification.

The same applies to the Imam (الإمام) in the sense used by the Quran. The Exalted describes him with patience and certainty (اليقين), as in His saying: **وَجَعَلْنا مِنْهُمْ أَئِمَّةً يَهْدُونَ بِأَمْرِنا لَمَّا صَبَرُوا وَكانُوا بِآياتِنا يُوقِنُونَ** (And We appointed from among them imams to guide by Our command, when they had been patient and had conviction in Our signs) [32:24]. He identifies them by the lifting of the veil for them, as in His saying: **وَكَذلِكَ نُرِي إِبْراهِيمَ مَلَكُوتَ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَلِيَكُونَ مِنَ الْمُوقِنِينَ** (Thus did We show Abraham the dominion of the heavens and the earth, that he might be of those who have conviction) [6:75], and His saying: **كَلَّا لَوْ تَعْلَمُونَ عِلْمَ الْيَقِينِ لَتَرَوُنَّ الْجَحِيمَ** (No indeed! Were you to know with certain knowledge, you would surely see hell) [102:6-7]. Discussion on this has preceded in some earlier sections.

As for the angels (الملائكة), what they carry of the heavenly revelation before its descent, as well as what they witness of the realm of the dominion (عالم الملكوت), is "witnessed" (شهادة) in relation to them, even if it is "unseen" (غيب) in relation to us. Furthermore, His saying: "He does not disclose His Unseen to anyone" only includes the people of this world who live upon the surface of the earth. Otherwise, it would be invalidated by the dead who witness the affairs of the Hereafter, which is from the Unseen by the text of the Quran. Thus, not a single individual would remain under the general negation, for there is no one who will not be resurrected—"that is a day on which mankind will be gathered, and that is a day to be witnessed." Just as the plane of existence (نشأة) of the dead is different from that of this world, so too the plane of existence of the angels is different from that of matter.

Third: His saying: "then He dispatches a guard before him and behind him" until the end of the two verses indicates that divine revelation is protected from the moment of its issuance from the source of revelation until its delivery to the people, and is safeguarded on the path of its descent until it reaches the one for whom it was intended.

As for its safeguard from the time of its issuance from its source until it reaches the Messenger, His saying "behind him" (١) is sufficient to indicate this. As for its safeguard during the Messenger's reception and acquisition of it from the angel of revelation—such that he recognizes it and does not err in receiving it—and its safeguard in his memory—such that he retains it exactly as it was revealed without forgetting, changing, or altering it—and its safeguard in his delivery of it to the people from the interference of Satan, the evidence for this is His saying: "so that He may know that they have delivered the messages of their Lord." This indicates that the divine purpose for dispatching the guard is to know their delivery of the messages of their Lord—that is, for the delivery of the revelation to the people to be realized in the external world. This necessitates its reaching them. Were the Messenger not protected in all three aforementioned aspects, the divine purpose would not be fulfilled, which is obvious. Since the Exalted mentioned no way to achieve this purpose other than dispatching the guard, it indicates that the revelation is guarded by angels while it is with the Messenger, just as it is guarded by them on its path to the Messenger until it reaches him. This is reinforced by His subsequent saying: "and He encompasses all that is with them."

As for its safeguard on its journey from the Messenger until it reaches the people, His saying "before him" is sufficient, based on the meaning previously explained.

Add to this the indication of His saying: "so that He may know that they have delivered the messages of their Lord," following the approach of its indication mentioned earlier.

From this clarification branches the fact that the Messenger is aided by inerrancy (العصمة) in receiving the revelation from his Lord, in preserving it, and in delivering it to the people—protected from error in all three aspects. This is because the verse indicates that what Allah has sent down of His religion to the people through the path of the Message via revelation is protected in all its stages until it reaches the people. Among its stages are the Messenger's reception of the revelation, his preservation of it, and his delivery of it to the people.

Delivery (التبليغ) encompasses both word and deed, for there is delivery in action just as there is in word. Thus, the Messenger is inerrant (معصوم) from disobedience by committing prohibited acts or neglecting religious obligations, because in doing so there would be a delivery of that which contradicts the religion. He is therefore inerrant in action just as he is inerrant from error in receiving the revelation, preserving it, and delivering it verbally.

It has been previously noted that prophethood (النبوة), like the Message (الرسالة), revolves around revelation. Thus, the prophet is like the messenger in the quality of inerrancy. The result is that the recipients of revelation, whether they be messengers or prophets, are inerrant in receiving the revelation, in preserving what was revealed to them, and in delivering it to the people through word and deed.

Fourth: That which is excepted in the verse from the disclosure of the Unseen is the disclosure to the Messenger of that upon which the realization of the delivery of his message depends. This is broader than just the text of the message—such as the doctrinal knowledge (المعارف الاعتقادية), the laws of religion, stories, lessons, wisdom, and exhortations—and includes the signs of the message and the miracles (المعجزات) indicating the truthfulness of the Messenger in his claim. Examples include what is recounted of some messengers informing of unseen events, such as Salih's saying to his people: **تَمَتَّعُوا فِي دارِكُمْ ثَلاثَةَ أَيَّامٍ ذلِكَ وَعْدٌ غَيْرُ مَكْذُوبٍ** (‘Enjoy yourselves in your homes for three days: that is a promise not to be belied’) [11:65], and Jesus's saying to the Children of Israel: **وَأُنَبِّئُكُمْ بِما تَأْكُلُونَ وَما تَدَّخِرُونَ فِي بُيُوتِكُمْ إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآيَةً لَكُمْ** (‘I will also inform you of what you eat and what you store up in your houses. There is indeed a sign in that for you’) [3:49]. Likewise, the promises of the messengers and the prophecies (الملاحم) mentioned in the Mighty Book are all instances of their being disclosed the Unseen.

---
(١) This is based on the pronoun referring to the Messenger. As for the possibility of the pronoun referring to the Unseen, the indicator would be the combination of "before him and behind him," but this is weak as previously mentioned.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *Tafsir al-Ayyashi*, it is narrated from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) that al-Mu'tasim asked him about the thief: from which place must the hand be cut? He (peace be upon him) said: "The cutting must be from the joint of the base of the fingers, leaving the palm."

He asked: "And what is the proof for that?" He replied: "The saying of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): 'Prostration is upon seven parts: the face, the two hands, the two knees, and the two feet.' If the hand were cut from the wrist or the elbow, he would not be left with a hand to prostrate upon. And Allah has said: **وَأَنَّ الْمَساجِدَ لِلَّهِ** (And that the mosques belong to Allah) [72:18], meaning by it these seven organs upon which one prostrates—**فَلا تَدْعُوا مَعَ اللهِ أَحَداً** (so do not invoke anyone along with Allah). And that which belongs to Allah is not to be cut." (The hadith).

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of narration from Hammad ibn 'Isa, from Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him) in a hadith: "He—meaning Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him)—prostrated upon eight bones: the two palms, the two knees, the two big toes, the forehead, and the nose." He said: "Seven of them are obligatory (فرض) to prostrate upon—and they are the ones Allah mentioned in His Book when He said: **وَأَنَّ الْمَساجِدَ لِلَّهِ فَلا تَدْعُوا مَعَ اللهِ أَحَداً** (And that the mosques belong to Allah, so do not invoke anyone along with Allah). They are the forehead, the two palms, the two knees, and the two big toes. Placing the nose on the ground is a recommended practice (سنة)."

In *al-Khara'ij wa al-Jara'ih*, Muhammad ibn al-Fadl al-Hashimi narrated from Imam al-Rida (peace be upon him) that he looked at Ibn Hadhab and said: "If I were to inform you that you will be afflicted in these days by the blood of a relative of yours, would you believe me?" He said: "No, for the Unseen is known by none but Allah the Exalted." He (peace be upon him) said: "Does He not say: **عالِمُ الْغَيْبِ فَلا يُظْهِرُ عَلى غَيْبِهِ أَحَداً ـ إِلَّا مَنِ ارْتَضى مِنْ رَسُولٍ** (Knower of the Unseen, He does not disclose His Unseen to anyone, except to an apostle He approves of)? The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) is approved by Allah, and we are the heirs of that Messenger whom Allah informed of whatever He willed of His Unseen. Thus, we know what was and what will be until the Day of Resurrection."

**I say:** The reports in this chapter are beyond counting, and their import is that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) received it through revelation from his Lord, and they [the Imams] received it through inheritance from him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم).
